svg.title: Gray-Scott initialization sensitivity
svg.desc: The same feed-vs-kill grid after 1400 steps under three bounded seed profiles: centered square, split twin patches, and annulus shell.
svg.text: The same feed-vs-kill grid after 1400 steps under three bounded seed profiles: centered square, split twin patches, and annulus shell.

text.tspan: Each cell shows max(active fraction) - min(active fraction) after swapping only the seed profile.
The same bounded seed swap, now measured through interface sharpness instead of fill fraction.
Largest active-profile swing: F=0.030, k=0.054, span=1.000 (centered square → split twin patches).
Largest edge-profile swing: F=0.026, k=0.054, span=0.0254.
Robust active counterexample: F=0.030, k=0.051 stays active under every profile with only 0.000 active span.

This is the bounded caveat the repo needed: some chemistry cells survive profile swaps almost unchanged, while others flip from near-extinction to broad occupancy once the seeded patch is split or hollowed.
The three profiles were kept comparable but not identical on purpose.
This is a seed-geometry audit, not a claim that one universal initialization already exists.
F=0.030, k=0.054 has the widest active-fraction span (1.000) across the three bounded seed profiles.
F=0.030, k=0.051 stays chemically active under every profile while keeping one of the smallest combined active/edge drifts.
Read the heatmaps first, then the spotlight rows.
The top cells tell you where profile swaps matter most; the thumbnails show what those swings actually look like in the final V field instead of leaving the note at one scalar drift score.
